blocks:
Ayahs

أَلْهَىٰكُمُ ٱلتَّكَاثُرُ (١)
Rivalry [and vainglory] distracted you (1)
حَتَّىٰ زُرْتُمُ ٱلْمَقَابِرَ (٢)
until you visited [even] the graves. (2)
كَلَّا سَوْفَ تَعْلَمُونَ (٣)
No indeed, soon you will know! (3)
ثُمَّ كَلَّا سَوْفَ تَعْلَمُونَ (٤)
No indeed, soon you will know for yet another time! (4)
كَلَّا لَوْ تَعْلَمُونَ عِلْمَ ٱلْيَقِينِ (٥)
Indeed, were you to know with certain knowledge, (5)
لَتَرَوُنَّ ٱلْجَحِيمَ (٦)
you would have surely seen hell [in this very life]. (6)
ثُمَّ لَتَرَوُنَّهَا عَيْنَ ٱلْيَقِينِ (٧)
Afterward you will surely see it with the eye of certainty. (7)
ثُمَّ لَتُسْـَٔلُنَّ يَوْمَئِذٍ عَنِ ٱلنَّعِيمِ (٨)
Then, on that day, you will surely be questioned concerning the Blessing. (8)

(Commentary)

This is a severe rebuke of people for their preoccupation with diversion (التلهي) through the mutual rivalry in amassing (التكاثر) wealth, children, and supporters, and for their heedlessness (الغفلة) regarding the consequence of loss and punishment that lies beyond it. It is a threat that they shall soon know and see this, and they shall be questioned concerning these blessings (النعم) which were bestowed upon them so that they might be grateful, yet they occupied themselves with them and exchanged the blessing of Allah for disbelief.

Based on its context, the Surah could be either Meccan or Medinan. What has been narrated regarding the occasion of its revelation (شأن النزول) will be mentioned in the "Discussion of Narrations," Allah willing.

His saying, the Exalted: أَلْهاكُمُ التَّكاثُرُ حَتَّى زُرْتُمُ الْمَقابِرَ — "Rivalry [and vainglory] distracted you (1) until you visited [even] the graves." It is stated in al-Mufradat: "Diversion (اللهو) is that which occupies a person away from what concerns and matters to him." He said: "It is said: 'Such a thing diverted him (ألهاه),' meaning it occupied him away from what was more important to him." He cited the verse: أَلْهاكُمُ التَّكاثُرُ (Rivalry distracted you). [End of quote].

He also stated: "Mutual rivalry (المكاثرة) and amassing (التكاثر) refer to competing in the abundance of wealth and glory." [End of quote]. He further said: "Al-Maqbarah (المقبرة)—with a kasrah on the mim—and al-Maqbarah—with a fathah—is the place of graves, and its plural is maqabir (مقابر). The verse حَتَّى زُرْتُمُ الْمَقابِرَ (until you visited the graves) is a metaphor for death." [End of quote].

Thus, the meaning according to the context is: your preoccupation with amassing the enjoyments and ornaments of the world, and your racing to increase your numbers and resources, has distracted you from what truly matters to you—which is the remembrance of Allah—until you met death, such that heedlessness (الغفلة) enveloped you throughout your lives.

It has also been said: The meaning is that you were occupied by boasting and competing in the number of men, such that one group would say, "We have more men," and the other would say, "We have more," until, having exhausted the count of the living, you went to the graves and counted the dead among your men, seeking to outnumber others through your deceased.

This meaning is based on what has been narrated regarding the occasions of revelation: that two tribes from the Ansar boasted against each other, first regarding the living and then the dead. In some reports, this occurred in Mecca between the clans of Banu Abd Manaf and Banu Sahm, and thus the Surah was revealed. The story will follow in the "Discussion of Narrations."

His saying, the Exalted: كَلَّا سَوْفَ تَعْلَمُونَ — "No indeed! Soon you will know!" This is a deterrent (الردع) against their preoccupation with what does not concern them over what truly matters, and a declaration of their error. His saying: سَوْفَ تَعْلَمُونَ (Soon you will know) is a threat whose meaning, as suggested by the context, is: you shall soon know the consequence of this diversion of yours and recognize it when you are severed from the life of this world.

His saying, the Exalted: ثُمَّ كَلَّا سَوْفَ تَعْلَمُونَ — "Again, no indeed! Soon you will know!" This is an emphasis (التأكيد) of the preceding deterrent and threat. It has been said that the first refers to their knowledge at the time of death, and the second to their knowledge at the time of Resurrection (البعث).

His saying, the Exalted: كَلَّا لَوْ تَعْلَمُونَ عِلْمَ الْيَقِينِ لَتَرَوُنَّ الْجَحِيمَ — "No indeed! Were you to know with certain knowledge (5) you would surely see hell." This is a deterrent after a deterrent for emphasis. Certainty (اليقين) is knowledge (العلم) that is not permeated by doubt or suspicion.

His saying: لَوْ تَعْلَمُونَ عِلْمَ الْيَقِينِ (Were you to know with certain knowledge)—the apodosis (جواب) of the conditional "if" (لو) is omitted. The implied meaning is: "If you knew the matter with certain knowledge, what you know would have distracted you from boasting and competing in numbers." His saying: لَتَرَوُنَّ الْجَحِيمَ (you would surely see hell) is a new sentence (استئناف), and the lam is for an oath (القسم). The meaning is: "I swear, you shall surely see the Hellfire which is the recompense for this diversion." This is how they have interpreted it.

They said: It is not permissible for لَتَرَوُنَّ الْجَحِيمَ to be the apodosis of the counterfactual "if" (لو الامتناعية), because the seeing is a certain event that will occur, whereas the apodosis of such a conditional is not so.

This interpretation is based on the assumption that the "seeing of hell" refers to the Day of Resurrection (يوم القيامة), as He said: وَبُرِّزَتِ الْجَحِيمُ لِمَنْ يَرى — "and hell is brought into view for him who sees" (79:36). However, this is not conceded; rather, it is apparent that the intended meaning is seeing it before the Day of Resurrection with the vision of insight (رؤية البصيرة), which is the vision of the heart (رؤية القلب) that is among the effects of certainty (اليقين). This is indicated by His saying, the Exalted: وَكَذلِكَ نُرِي إِبْراهِيمَ مَلَكوتَ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ وَلِيَكُونَ مِنَ الْمُوقِنِينَ — "Thus did We show Abraham the dominion of the heavens and the earth, that he might be of those who possess certitude" (6:75). We have already discussed this previously. This heart-based vision before the Day of Resurrection is not realized for these diverted ones; rather, it is impossible for them because certainty (اليقين) is impossible for them.

His saying, the Exalted: ثُمَّ لَتَرَوُنَّها عَيْنَ الْيَقِينِ — "Then you will surely see it with the eye of certainty." What is meant by "the eye of certainty" (عين اليقين) is certainty itself. The meaning is: you shall see it with pure certainty, and this occurs through witnessing it on the Day of Resurrection. Evidence for this is His saying afterward: ثُمَّ لَتُسْئَلُنَّ يَوْمَئِذٍ عَنِ النَّعِيمِ (Then, on that day, you will surely be questioned about the blessings). Thus, the first seeing refers to seeing it before the Day of Resurrection, and the second refers to seeing it on the Day of Resurrection.

It has also been said: The first is before entering it on the Day of Resurrection, and the second is when they have entered it.

It has also been said: The first is through recognition (المعرفة) and the second through witnessing (المشاهدة). It has also been said: What is meant is seeing after seeing, indicating continuity and eternity (الخلود). There are other views as well, but they are weak.

His saying, the Exalted: ثُمَّ لَتُسْئَلُنَّ يَوْمَئِذٍ عَنِ النَّعِيمِ — "Then, on that day, you will surely be questioned about the blessings." The apparent context is that this address, as well as the preceding addresses in the Surah, is directed at people insofar as there are those among them who occupied themselves with the blessing (النعمة) of their Lord away from their Lord, such that mutual rivalry in these blessings made them forget the remembrance of Allah. The rebuke and threat in the Surah are directed at people in general outwardly, but they truly fall upon a specific group among them: those whom rivalry distracted.

Similarly, the apparent context is that "the blessing" (النعيم) is meant in an absolute sense (مطلقة), encompassing everything that can be called a blessing. Thus, man is responsible for every blessing Allah has bestowed upon him.

This is because a blessing (النعمة)—which is a matter that suits the one blessed and contains for him a type of good and benefit—only becomes a blessing in relation to the recipient if he uses it in a way that leads to his felicity (السعادة) and benefit. However, if he uses it in a contrary manner, it becomes a bane (نقمة) for him, even if it is a blessing in itself.

Allah the Exalted created man and made the goal of his creation—which is his felicity and the ultimate end of his perfection—servile proximity (التقرب العبودي) to Him, as He said: وَما خَلَقْتُ الْجِنَّ وَالْإِنْسَ إِلَّا لِيَعْبُدُونِ — "I did not create the jinn and the humans except that they may worship Me" (51:56). This is the divine guardianship (الولاية الإلهية) over His servant. Allah, the Glorified, has prepared for him everything through which he may find felicity and benefit in his journey toward the goal for which he was created—these are the blessings (النعم). Thus, He showered His blessings upon him, both outward and inward.

Therefore, using these blessings in a manner that Allah approves of and which leads man to his desired goal is the path to reaching that goal—and that is obedience (الطاعة). Using them by becoming fixated on them and forgetting what lies beyond them is error (الغي), misguidance (الضلال), and severance from the goal—and that is disobedience (المعصية). He, the Glorified, has decreed a judgment that cannot be turned back or changed: that man shall return to Him, and He shall question him about his deeds, hold him to account, and recompense him. His deeds consist of his use of the divine blessings. He, the Exalted, said: وَأَنْ لَيْسَ لِلْإِنْسانِ إِلَّا ما سَعى وَأَنَّ سَعْيَهُ سَوْفَ يُرى ثُمَّ يُجْزاهُ الْجَزاءَ الْأَوْفى وَأَنَّ إِلى رَبِّكَ الْمُنْتَهى — "and that nothing belongs to man except what he strives for, (39) and that he will soon be shown his endeavour, (40) then he will be rewarded for it with the fullest reward, (41) and that the terminus is toward your Lord" (53:39-42). Thus, the questioning about the servant's deeds is a questioning about the blessings: how did he use them? Did he show gratitude (الشكر) for the blessing or did he show ingratitude (الكفر) toward it?

(Discussion of Narrations)

In al-Majma': It is said: It was revealed concerning the Jews; they said: "We are more numerous than the children of so-and-so, and the children of so-and-so are more numerous than the children of so-and-so." This distracted them until they died in misguidance. Narrated from Qatadah.

It is also said: It was revealed concerning a clan of the Ansar who boasted against each other. Narrated from Abu Buraydah. It is also said: It was revealed concerning two clans of the Quraysh: Banu Abd Manaf ibn Qusayy and Banu Sahm ibn Amr. They competed in numbers and counted their nobles, and Banu Abd Manaf outnumbered them. Then they said: "Let us count our dead," until they visited the graves and counted them, saying: "This is the grave of so-and-so, and this is the grave of so-and-so." Banu Sahm then outnumbered them because they were more numerous during the Jahiliyyah. Narrated from Muqatil and al-Kalbi.

In Tafsir al-Burhan, from al-Barqi, from his father, from Ibn Abi Umayr, from Hisham ibn Salim, from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) regarding the saying of the Exalted: لَوْ تَعْلَمُونَ عِلْمَ الْيَقِينِ (Were you to know with certain knowledge). He said: "It is witnessing (المعاينة)."

I say: The narration supports the meaning we previously established.

In Tafsir al-Qummi, with its chain of transmission from Jamil, from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him). He said: I said to him: لَتُسْئَلُنَّ يَوْمَئِذٍ عَنِ النَّعِيمِ (you will surely be questioned about the blessings). He said: "This nation shall be questioned about what Allah blessed them with through His Messenger, and then through his Ahl al-Bayt."

In al-Kafi, with its chain of transmission from Abu Khalid al-Kabuli. He said: I entered upon Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) and he called for food. I ate food with him—I have never eaten food more delicious or more delicate than it. When we finished the food, he said: "O Abu Khalid, how did you find your food?" or he said, "our food?" I said: "May I be your ransom, I have never eaten food more delicious or cleaner than it—but I remembered the verse in the Book of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: ثُمَّ لَتُسْئَلُنَّ يَوْمَئِذٍ عَنِ النَّعِيمِ (Then, on that day, you will surely be questioned about the blessings)." Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) said: "He will only question you about the Truth (الحق) you are upon."

In the same source, with its chain of transmission from Abu Hamzah. He said: We were a group with Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him), and he called for food the likes of which we had never experienced in terms of deliciousness and pleasantness. We were brought dates so clear and beautiful that we could see our faces in them. A man said: "You will surely be questioned about this blessing (النعيم) which you have enjoyed in the presence of the son of the Messenger of Allah." Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) said: "Indeed, Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, is more generous and more majestic than to feed you food, make it wholesome for you, and then question you about it. He will only question you about what He blessed you with through Muhammad and the Progeny of Muhammad صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله."

I say: This meaning is narrated from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (peace be upon them) through other paths and with different expressions. In some of them, it is stated: "The blessing (النعيم) is our Wilayah (ولاية), the Ahl al-Bayt." The meaning reverts to what we previously established regarding the generality of "the blessing" for every blessing Allah has bestowed, insofar as it is a blessing.

The explanation of this is that if any of these blessings were to be questioned, they are not questioned insofar as they are meat, bread, dates, or cold water, or that they are hearing, sight, hand, or foot, for example. Rather, they are questioned insofar as they are a blessing (نعمة) that Allah created for man and placed on the path of his perfection and the attainment of servile proximity, as previously indicated, and urged him to use them in gratitude (شكرا), not ingratitude (كفرا).

Thus, what is questioned is the blessing insofar as it is a blessing. It is well known that the one who points to the "blessing-ness" of the blessing and the manner of using it in gratitude, and the one who explains all of that, is the religion (الدين) brought by the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, and for whose explanation the Imams from his Ahl al-Bayt were appointed. Therefore, the questioning about the blessing refers back to the questioning about acting according to the religion in every movement and stillness. It is also well known that the questioning about the blessing—which is the religion—is a questioning about the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and the Imams after him, whose obedience Allah made mandatory and whose following He made obligatory in the journey toward Allah, the path of which is the use of blessings as explained by the Messenger and the Imams.

The fact that the questioning about the blessing is a questioning about the religion is indicated by what appears in the narration of Abu Khalid: "He will only question you about the Truth (الحق) you are upon."

The fact that it is a questioning about the blessing which is the Prophet and his Ahl al-Bayt is indicated by what appears in the two previous narrations of Jamil and Abu Hamzah: "This nation shall be questioned about what Allah blessed them with through His Messenger, and then through his Ahl al-Bayt," or similar meanings. In some narrations: "The blessing (النعيم) is the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله—Allah blessed the people of the world through him and rescued them from misguidance." In some: "The blessing is our Wilayah, the Ahl al-Bayt." The essence is the same, and part of the Wilayah of the Ahl al-Bayt is the obligation of obeying them and following them in the path of servitude (العبودية) that they traverse.

In al-Majma': It is said: The blessing (النعيم) is health (الصحة) and free time (الفراغ). Narrated from Ikrimah. This is supported by what Ibn Abbas narrated from the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, who said: "Two blessings in which many people are cheated: health and free time."

In the same source: It is said: It—meaning the blessing—is security (الأمن) and health. Narrated from Abdullah ibn Mas'ud and Mujahid. This has also been narrated from Abu Ja'far and Abu Abdillah (peace be upon them).

I say: In other narrations from Sunni sources, the blessing is said to be dates and cold water, and in some, other things. All of these should be understood as providing examples.

In a Prophetic tradition from their sources as well: "There are three things for which the servant will not be questioned: a rag with which he covers his nakedness, a morsel with which he staves off his hunger, or a house that shelters him from heat and cold." This tradition should be understood as referring to the lightness of the reckoning (الحساب) regarding necessities and the negation of rigorous scrutiny therein. And Allah knows best.
